# Releasing

Releases are cut by the [`Release`](workflows/release.yml) workflow: it bumps the version,
publishes the package to npm and opens a GitHub release whose body is the changelog built
from the commits since the previous tag.

## One-time setup

1. **`NPM_TOKEN` secret** — create an _Automation_ access token on npmjs.com (Access Tokens →
   Generate New Token → Granular/Automation, with publish rights on the package) and add it under
   _Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret_ with the name `NPM_TOKEN`.
2. **Workflow write permissions** — _Settings → Actions → General → Workflow permissions_ must be
   set to _Read and write permissions_, otherwise the workflow cannot push the release commit and
   the tag.
3. If the default branch is protected, allow `github-actions[bot]` to bypass the protection,
   or the final push will be rejected.

## Cutting a release

_Actions → Release → Run workflow_, on the default branch, then pick:

| Input          | Meaning                                                                          |
| -------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `release_type` | `auto` (default), `patch`, `minor` or `major`                                    |
| `version`      | explicit version, e.g. `23.0.1`; overrides `release_type` when set               |
| `dry_run`      | run the whole pipeline but skip npm publish, the git push and the GitHub release |

`auto` resolves to `minor` when there is at least one `feat:` commit since the last tag, and to
`patch` otherwise. **It never resolves to `major`**: the major of this library tracks the Angular
major (22, 23, …), so it is never derived from the commits — a breaking change only shows up as a
warning in the log. To move to a new major, run the workflow with `release_type: major` or, when
the target does not match a plain `major` bump, type the whole version into `version`
(e.g. `23.0.1`).

Use `dry_run` first when in doubt: the run uploads the generated `RELEASE_NOTES.md`, `CHANGELOG.md`
and the `.tgz` tarball as an artifact, leaving npm and the repository untouched.

## What the workflow does

1. refuses to run outside the default branch;
2. installs with `npm ci`, then runs the format check, the linter, the tests and the builds —
   a failure here aborts the release before anything is published;
3. bumps the root `package.json` and the library `package.json` to the same version;
4. fails if that version is already on npm;
5. writes `RELEASE_NOTES.md` (release body) and prepends the same content to `CHANGELOG.md`;
6. publishes `dist/ng-http-caching` to npm with
   [provenance](https://docs.npmjs.com/generating-provenance-statements);
7. commits `chore(release): vX.Y.Z [skip ci]`, tags `vX.Y.Z` and pushes both;
8. creates the GitHub release from the generated notes.

## Changelog

The changelog is generated by [`scripts/release.mjs`](scripts/release.mjs) from the
[conventional commits](https://www.conventionalcommits.org/) since the previous `v*` tag, so commit
subjects should look like `type(scope): subject` — `feat:`, `fix:`, `perf:`, `refactor:`, `docs:`,
`test:`, `build:`, `ci:`, `style:`, `chore:`, `revert:`. Anything else still shows up, under
_Other changes_. A `!` after the type (`feat!:`) or a `BREAKING CHANGE:` line in the body promotes
the commit to the _BREAKING CHANGES_ section on top of the notes.

The same commands the workflow runs are available locally to preview the next release:

```bash
npm run release:bump -- --type auto
npm run release:notes -- --version 23.0.1
```

## Manual fallback

`publish.bat` still performs the same steps from a local machine (npm login, patch bump, build,
publish) for the cases where the workflow cannot be used. It does not generate any changelog or
GitHub release.
